source.title: Forecasting: Principles and Practice, the Pythonic Way
source.citation: Hyndman, R.J., Athanasopoulos, G., Garza, A., Challu, C., Mergenthaler, M., & Olivares, K.G. (2025). Forecasting: Principles and Practice, the Pythonic Way. OTexts: Melbourne, Australia. Available at: OTexts.com/fpppy. Accessed on 28 April 2026.

principles_to_enforce.fpp_guidance: Prepare data, identify missing values, filter/repair time series, and understand data features before estimating models.
Graphical/diagnostic methods are central for understanding data and model validity.
principles_to_enforce.runtime_receipt: Model card headline, warnings, and best-practice receipts

principles_to_enforce.fpp_guidance: Simple benchmark forecasts such as mean, naive, drift, and seasonal naive are surprisingly effective and should be used as references.
Forecast methods should be checked with time-ordered validation and accuracy measures.
Prediction intervals are part of practical forecasting but should be presented only when model/history support them.
Holiday/event effects and known future changes require explicit variables or assumptions rather than blind extrapolation.
Parent and child forecasts must be checked for coherence before being presented together.
Forecast combinations can be useful, but weights must be based on validation evidence and exposed for review.
Forecasting workflows should be reproducible and inspectable; failures need enough context to debug data, model, and assumption issues.
